subject: ap-chemistry
title: Molecular Geometry, Hydrogen Bonding & Phase Changes

parts.prompt: Identify the hybridization of the valence orbitals of the C atom in the 2 molecule.
parts.scoring_notes: The question refers to H₂CO (formaldehyde). The C atom has 3 electron domains (2 single bonds + 1 double bond), giving sp² hybridization.

alternatives.correct_example: The carbon atom in H₂CO (formaldehyde) has 3 electron domains: two C–H single bonds and one C=O double bond. Three electron domains correspond to sp² hybridization.

wrong_examples: sp³ — that would require 4 single bonds with no double bond.
sp — that requires only 2 electron domains (linear geometry).
common_traps: Counting the C=O bond as two separate domains — a double bond counts as one electron domain for hybridization and geometry.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 01: For the correct answer: sp².

sample_responses.response: $\text{sp}^2$. The C atom in \ce{H2CO} has 3 electron domains: two C–H bonds and one C=O double bond (counted as one domain). Three electron domains → sp² hybridization.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies sp² hybridization and explains that the C=O double bond counts as one electron domain. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: $\text{sp}^3$. The C atom forms 4 bonds (2 C–H and 2 bonds in C=O).
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrectly counts the C=O double bond as two separate electron domains. A double bond is a single electron domain for VSEPR and hybridization purposes. sp³ requires 4 electron domains. No credit.

parts.prompt:
In the diagram provided, draw a SINGLE dashed line (----) to represent a strong hydrogen-
CH OH H CO
bonding attraction between one 3 molecule and one 2 molecule in the mixture.
CH OH H CO
parts.scoring_notes: The dashed line must connect the O atom of H₂CO (acceptor) with the H atom of the –OH group of CH₃OH (donor). Do not accept H–H or O–O connections.

scoring_points.description: For a correct dashed line showing hydrogen bond from O(H₂CO) to H–O(CH₃OH): 1 point

required_elements: Dashed line connects the O atom of H₂CO to the H atom in the –OH group of CH₃OH.
alternatives.correct_example: The hydrogen bond forms between the lone pair on the C=O oxygen of formaldehyde (hydrogen bond acceptor) and the O–H hydrogen of methanol (hydrogen bond donor): O(H₂CO)····H–O(CH₃OH).

wrong_examples: Drawing the dashed line from the C of H₂CO — only the O can accept a hydrogen bond.
Drawing the dashed line from the C–H of CH₃OH — C–H bonds do not participate in conventional hydrogen bonding.
common_traps: Hydrogen bonding requires N–H, O–H, or F–H as donor; and N, O, or F as acceptor.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 02: For a correct diagram: The diagram should show a dashed line between the O atom in one H₂CO molecule and the H atom in the –OH group of one CH₃OH molecule.

sample_responses.response: [Draws a dashed line from the C=O oxygen of H₂CO to the O–H hydrogen of CH₃OH: O(H₂CO)····H–O(CH₃OH)]
sample_responses.commentary: The hydrogen bond acceptor is the carbonyl oxygen of H₂CO (lone pair on O accepts the H), and the donor is the O–H of methanol. This is the correct connectivity. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: [Draws a dashed line from the C of H₂CO to the O of CH₃OH]
sample_responses.commentary: Carbon is not a hydrogen bond acceptor. The dashed line must connect the O atom of H₂CO to the H atom of the –OH group in CH₃OH. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The scientist plans to cool a gaseous mixture of 3 and 2 to form a liquid mixture
and finds data on the two compounds. The data are summarized in the table.
Melting Point Boiling Point Enthalpy of Vaporization
Substance (K) (K) (
kJ/mol
)
CH OH
3 176 338 37.6
H $CO_2$ 181 254 24.2
CH OH
i. Propose a temperature to which the mixture should be cooled such that 3 and
H $CO_2$ will both be liquids.
ii. The scientist analyzes the mixture after it is cooled and determines that 8.59 g of
CH
OH(l)
is present. Calculate the amount of thermal energy, in kJ, that was removed to
condense the 8.59 g of
CH
OH
(molar mass 32.04
g/mol
) at its boiling point.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): both substances are liquid between their respective melting and boiling points. The overlap range where both are liquid is 181 K–254 K (H₂CO boiling point is lower than CH₃OH boiling point). Any temperature in 181–254 K range is acceptable. Part (c)(ii): q = n × ΔH_vap; sign must be negative (energy removed).

scoring_points.description: For a proposed temperature in the range 181 K–254 K and correct q calculation: 1 point

required_elements: (i) Any temperature between 181 K and 254 K (inclusive) is acceptable.
(ii) $q = \frac{8.59\text{ g}}{32.04\text{ g/mol}} \times 37.6\text{ kJ/mol} = 10.1\text{ kJ}$ removed (energy released = −10.1 kJ)
alternatives.correct_example:
(i) A temperature of 220 K would make both substances liquid, since 181 K < 220 K < 254 K. 
(ii) $n(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) = 8.59/32.04 = 0.268\text{ mol}$ 
$q = 0.268\text{ mol} \times 37.6\text{ kJ/mol} = 10.1\text{ kJ removed}$

wrong_examples: Proposing a temperature above 338 K — CH₃OH would be gaseous.
Proposing a temperature below 176 K — CH₃OH would be solid.
common_traps: H₂CO has a lower boiling point (254 K) than CH₃OH (338 K); the upper bound for both being liquid is 254 K.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Points 03–04: Point 03: For a correct proposal: The proposed temperature should be in the range 181 K–254 K. Point 04: For the correct calculated value: $8.59\text{ g CH}_3\text{OH} \times \frac{1\text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH}}{32.04\text{ g CH}_3\text{OH}} \times \frac{-37.6\text{ kJ}}{1\text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH}} = -10.1\text{ kJ}$, so 10.1 kJ are removed.

sample_responses.response: (i) 220 K. Both \ce{CH3OH} (bp 338 K, mp 176 K) and \ce{H2CO} (bp 254 K, mp 181 K) are liquids between 181 K and 254 K, and 220 K is in that range. (ii) $n(\text{CH}_3\text{OH}) = 8.59/32.04 = 0.268\text{ mol}$; $q = 0.268\text{ mol} \times 37.6\text{ kJ/mol} = 10.1\text{ kJ removed}$.
sample_responses.commentary: Proposes a valid temperature in the 181–254 K liquid range for both substances and correctly calculates the heat removed using ΔH_vap. Earns both sub-points.
300 K exceeds the boiling point of H₂CO (254 K), so H₂CO would be gaseous at that temperature — both substances must be liquid. The temperature is out of range. No credit for part (i).
